---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

class: slideblue

.size80[**Today's Agenda**]

<br>

.size45[
What are the Complicating Factors in Environmental Policymaking?

+ Uncertainty, Time Horizons and Discount Rates
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2022)
]]

???

## Prep for Class
1. Old US Public policy notes (14.2) covered temporal discounting
2. Prep email with link to Google sheet



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06-1-risk_meter.jpeg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Last week we discussed how different perceptions of risk among stakeholders can complicate environmental problem-solving.

### Any questions on the material from last week?

#### - Give me an example of a situation from your day-to-day in which you are risk averse.

#### - Give me an example of a situation from your day-to-day life in which you are risk acceptant.

<br>

On Thursday we discussed real-world case studies.

### What were our best ideas for addressing environmental problems where stakeholders hold different levels of risk acceptance? 




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06-1-time_horizons_sharks.webp')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

This week we shift to our second set of environmental problem complications.

The decisions we make day-to-day carry impacts that differ depending on how you evaluate them according to time.

Here we see an investor surrounded by sharks but with his eyes on the distant horizon.

A choice to deal with the sharks might push you off course and away from your distant goals.

A choice to focus on the horizon might lead to serious short term costs as you run into each shark.

The first takeaway is that there is no "right" choice, BUT failing to consider how stakeholders differ in their time horizons will make their behavior impossible to understand or adjust.  



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/05-1-Discounting.webp')
background-size: 90%
background-position: center

class: middle

???

Economists and policy analysts have developed a concept, discounting, that is mean to help us think about different time horizons.

Discounting simply means: To count at less than face value.

The investor focused entirely on maneuvering his metaphorical boat around the sharks will never reach his destination.

+ This is someone who heavily discounts the future.

All people discount the future due to uncertainty but the amounts people discount by will vary considerably.

<br>

I'm hoping you covered this in some depth in Environmental Economics.

For those who didn't let's summarize the idea like this:



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06-1-popeye1.gif')
background-size: 75%
background-position: center

???

### Anybody watch popeye cartoons growing up?

He was a "sailor man" who got into scrapes starting in comic strips of the 1930s.

When he ate spinach he became super strong and then he'd inevitably beat the crap out of the problem.

I loved these cartoons as a kid and they definitely got me to eat my spinach.

I digress, this isn't actually about Popeye at all.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06-1-wimpy_burger.jpeg')
background-size: 50%
background-position: center

???

It's about Wimpy.

J Wellington Wimpy was the "straight man" to Popeye's character.

And he was a con man always trying to get people to pay for his beloved hamburgers.

The gag was that he would try to convince people to buy him a burger with the promise that he would pay them next week.

Hilarious joke during the great depression, right?

<br>

Setting aside the depressing nature of old timey cartoons, let's bring this back to the discount rate.

Assuming he intends to pay at all, the key here is that Wimpy believes that a dollar next week is worth less than a dollar today.

This makes sense at times of high inflation.

If prices are rising then goods will cost more as time goes by.

So, get a burger today and pay for it with a dollar from next week when the burgers will cost $2!

<br>

The more depressing version of this assumes Wimpy has lost all hope and isn't sure he'll survive to next week.

+ Eat today because tomorrow isn't coming.

<br>

In either version, Wimpy is demonstrating for us how a discount rate impacts decision-making.

People may discount the future for many reasons and the level of that discount impacts their present decision-making.




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

???

Let's play with this idea and see if we can identify your personal discount rates.

Everybody take out a piece of paper or something you can write on.

I'm going to ask you a series of questions, reflect on them and then write down your answer to each one.

Ok, question 1.

Someone offers you a choice.

You can have $10 right now OR you can wait a year and receive more than $10.


--

.size55[**Make a Choice:**]

<br>

.size45[Q1. $10 now or wait one year for $X?]

???

Here's the question!

What is the smallest amount of money they could offer you that would get you to skip the $10 and wait for the bigger payment next year?

I assume an offer of $11 isn't nearly enough to get you to wait and an offer of $10 billion gets everyone to wait.

The question here is what is the smallest amount you would demand to wait for the money?

### Does everybody understand the choice they are making?

Ok, write down your answer.

--

<br>

.size45[Q2. $1,000 now or wait one year for $X?]

???

Q2 - What if the offer was $1,000 right now? 

What is the smallest amount of money they could offer you that would get you to skip the immediate payment and wait until next year?

Think about this for a moment and write down your answer.

--

<br>

.size45[Q3. $1 million now or wait one year for $X?]

???

Ok, last one.

How much would they have to offer you to get you to pass up the chance at 1 million dollars right now?

<br>

*Gather answers ON THE BOARD, one row per student*

<br>

Ok, this is our dataset.

The variation in responses at each level and across the levels tells us something important about your discount rates.

### So, what do we learn about how our class values, or doesn't value, the future?

#### - How many different types of discount rate are evident in our class?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06-1-lightbulb.jpeg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

Let's take this table as our "outcome" to explain.

### Based on this data and our discussions, what mechanisms could we propose to explain why some people have high vs low discount rates?

*Brainstorming Hypotheses*

<br>

Now, let's take our talk about time horizons and discount rates out of the realm of benefits and into the realm of environmental costs.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/06-1-flooding.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

???

The discount rate tells us how much you discount future gains and future losses! 

In environmental policy terms discount rates are helpful for thinking about avoiding harms.

In other words, what would you pay to reduce a small harm today that may become a big harm in the future?

- e.g. if the worst effects of climate change are astronomically expensive but they won't reach us for 50 years how much are you willing to pay today to avert them?

### Make sense?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-forest.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.size45[
Pindyck, R. S. (2007). Uncertainty in Environmental Economics. *Review of Environmental Economics and Policy*. 1(1), 45–65.
]

<br>

.center[
.size45[**What are the key lessons we can use to overcome uncertainty and solve environmental problems?**]
]

???

*Seminar discussion of the lessons from the article*

Uncertainty is a hugely important element of policy design when it comes to environmental problems. Emphasize the lessons for good policy design!

- The Uncertain Nature of Benefits and Costs (49-54)

- Irreversibilities (54-60)

- Discounting over Long Time Horizons (60-62)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-green_blue_swirl_side.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

class: middle

.size60[**For Thursday**]

.size40[
Find us a **recent, real world example** of competing estimates of **uncertainty, time horizons or discount rates** complicating environmental problem-solving. 

Be ready to help us **brainstorm strategies** for addressing these complications.
]

???

You don't have to find a case that mirrors the problem you've selected for your paper.

HOWEVER, that's not a bad idea!

My aim is that we broaden and deepen our understanding of risk profiles complicating environmental problem-solving.

So, go find us good cases we can dig our teeth into.

**Submit the citation to your case before class.**

Be ready to present your case to the class AND start thinking about strategies we can use to address these risk based problems.

### Questions?
